sentences.text: Got married.
And I still was hided in Luck-- L-U-C-K-- the-- Luck, you have to explain it to you.
It's L-U-C-K.
The river-- Luck-- L-U-- Luck is in the map.
Luck in the map, and this, it was called the Slutsk.
They call it 'Lutsk.'
And then, which I'm trying to tell you, how I get this thing over here, broken down.
How I get this over here.
Where it came from.
Then I finished the leather, with the Russian passed away.
Now, the Germans came over here, and Prosajner, the head from the [NON-ENGLISH], that mean the [NON-ENGLISH] like-- we have--
Germans came in 1941, yeah.
Under Russian-- the Russian-- you have the leather and the remain for the-- then we didn't know what to do with the leather.
We have-- I have the leather-- everything registered.
And I-- not also registered, then come the [NON-ENGLISH] head from the city, Prosajner his name.
Prosajner.
Came to me.
He says, we have to do something with the leather.
I'm the boss of the leather.
Work it out.
I didn't give it to him.
Says, I can't do without anything.
You're the boss.
But you have to do it-- the Germans authority to it.
He says, I don't take the leather.
Just give me a little bit on the side.
I gave it to him.
And couple of weeks ago-- later, comes from the head, from the city [NON-ENGLISH] Poland.
Big city.
And that city has three, four cities and our city, Lokacze.
[NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], and all [NON-ENGLISH], comes-- belongs to that city.
And you come, you belong to us now.
You have to go to the city.
We give you a-- you go with me the city.
I took this leather with me and go to the main office, which is Horochow.
I told you.
Over there is a manager, Zimmerman.
The main officer is Winkler, a German from the-- from the [NON-ENGLISH], from the German government.
Winkler.
You have to put it on.
That Winkler was the main manager.
And the Jewish guy, Zimmerman, which has a parent-- relatives a lot over here.
If you're going to be in the paper, you're going to read it.
They probably-- they're going to talk about.
Then, I belong to them.
And I became the authority to make the leather and to bring them finished.
Raw material, which I cannot do it, like, foxes.
I couldn't do it.
Then the polizei master, Gretzner, gave me couple foxes.
And I took the foxes and bring to the city.
And they have-- and I gave-- I didn't give him the receipt.
But he says, next time, you bring me the receipt.
The next time he came, he wants-- was 1942, when I feel that a Jewish has to be killed-- to give me my receipt.
I say, [NON-ENGLISH].
Says, [SPEAKING NON-ENGLISH].
You speak Polish.
You speak Russian.
You speak a little bit German, [NON-ENGLISH].
He's in Germany.
I heard from this guy, he's in Germany.
He's still living.
Now, today, I don't know.
It's a long time ago.
Which one?
He says to me-- he says to me, cover up yourself.
You are speaking English well-- [NON-ENGLISH], you speak Russian well, and this.
And you could hide yourself.
I, if it comes a time to my when I have to hided, I have nowhere to go.
You could go anyplace you want to.
And you know the area.
He says to me-- that's what he said to me that time.
Yeah.
Later on--
When they--
--came in '41, I told you about it-- I-- they-- had a big store till I got to it.
But when I got to it, they start to work leather with the head, I told you, Prosajner.
And he came to me and says, work the leather.
And I finished the leather, and I bring it to the city.
And there, where I bring to the city, was there a sign.
Lokacze, Luck.
And the sign was broke down-- was wooden sign.
And-- in the front of me, going two Germans.
With-- we-- you know, the Germans, with-- says, where we are going, Luck or Lokacze?
We are no the-- direction.
Says, oh, here comes a Jude.
I had a big [NON-ENGLISH].
You know, the [NON-ENGLISH].
You're a Jude.
You're a Jew.
You was born here?
Say, sure, was raised over here.
Where's Luck?
L-U-C-K?
Is Luck?
In German, it's Luck.
In English, it's Luck.
But we called it 'Lutsk.' When I-- they tell me Luck, I say, so I tell you I was born here, but I don't remember where's Luck is.
Then, one guy says, [NON-ENGLISH], and he hit me over here and he put me over here-- without teeth.
Over here.
All over.
They fix me a little better.
Now I couldn't talk.
I couldn't do a thing.
Then they bring me back to the city of Lokacze.
It's 13 miles. 15 miles.
I says to the guy, you bring him the leather, to the gre-- to Winkler, Zimmerman.
And I'm going to go back to the-- came back to Lokacze.
Then the policeman say, take him to the hospital.
In the hospital, Dr.
Champen.
They operate.
They put me on simple-- they-- as they could.
They not operators.
And the doctor's living someplace around here, the Dr.
Champen.
Because I meet him 1944 in Lodz.
And we spoke to each other, and suddenly we disappear.
I don't know where it is.
Dr.
Champen, I wrote, he's in Chicago, several-- two letters, and then-- but if I-- you look all over, only in Russia, possibly he's there.
To the ghetto, it was six weeks after the Germans occupied, they-- they make a ghetto.
And they kill 10% from the Jewish people.
That was the law.
The city has 3,000 people, then they have to kill 300 people. 10%.
That was a law at that time.
I know what they say.
Then you got only 285 people.
And they kill them right around to the city because the city was-- the factory we were working was a little of water--
--and the other side-- huh?
In ghetto.
Yeah.
In Lokacze.
They killed 285 has missed 15 more.
They have-- they are.
No.
I-- I-- we heard it.
Because it's just over the water.
A little bit, we see it.
But they go-- and they-- I was protected because the police know don't touch from the fact-- from the [NON-ENGLISH], anybody.
I was there also between the people to kill.
But they took me out from there.
In the ghetto?
In the ghetto, later, was my mother, already, my brother.
Let me give you explanation how did I bring my brother come from the ghetto.
It was later on.
I need somebody to work, more workers, good workers.
I made application to [NON-ENGLISH] the-- to Winkler, to add from the factory.
I could not bring a man from here.
I took my brother with me.
I took him out from the basement, from the cellar, which he didn't see the light altogether.
Because it was after they did the killing.
I took him out with the [NON-ENGLISH]-- that means the head from the city.
We took him out, and I bring him to Lokacze.
And we work together with-- living together.
My brother.
And suddenly, my mother, after three, four weeks, she couldn't stay by herself.
She put down at night, 12 o'clock at night, the middle of the night.
And she walked to the-- the [NON-ENGLISH].
And she came to me, my mother.
We were together in the ghetto, Lokacze.
The ghetto was-- oh, Lokacze was-- the ghetto actually was 3,000 people.
It was maybe 2,500-something, or 3,000 people, 3,000.
The ghetto was a-- a big lot, you know, like a street, like over here is a street.
It was three, four streets.
But there was 3,000-- all everybody was in the ghetto.
Couple streets, yeah.
[INAUDIBLE] Yeah.
In the front-- the front market was-- doesn't belong.
But I had, in the front market windows, with the door, I marked finishing leather.
I bring from the factory the leather.
I work in the-- I went to the front.
I-- only I could go to the front.
The front.
But nobody else.
Now, let me give you another explanation.
My mother, when-- after the killing, the-- in the same city, 5,000-- 500 people, because that city, Horochow, was 5,000.
They kill-- have to kill 500.
Then my mother has friends.
The night where I do, she says, I going to see what could I do.
That the lady steal the border, goes here and goes there.
She has a husband over here and children over here.
She comes in.
And she says, you see my son?
Oh, your son, I see.
He goes with the-- with the head for the city.
He goes-- he goes nice dressed.
And your son is this and this and this and this.
Yeah.
She has a-- And when I bring leather, when I bring material, then I took material for flour or bread, I left it with my uncle over there.
And over there, I gave the other friends, I gave bread.
You know, bread, sugar, you know the way it is.
Other things, food.
Potatoes.
I give five kilo potatoes.
And I give their five.
Because the beginning, I start to bring from here to there.
Why should I gave over there?
I go with them there-- swap not legal.
But when I go with the two police, with the leather, see, then I steal then, the beginning, and later on, I have no problem.
I-- I give to them here, and I give-- meanwhile, it's my uncle Luchon Royce.
This was a rich man.
At that time, was rich.
And he haven't got brought to eat.
I gave him bread and potatoes.
And I give back them there.
And then most I didn't.
Regarded?
Guarded, was a little wire.
But nobody was going out the ghetto.
But if you'd go out the ghetto, this guy over here, they was watching.
When you go, he shoot.
Yeah.
Not fence.
Not fence.
It was an own wire and that.
So we know this is the wire.
This is the ghetto.
And that's it.
It's divided.
But in the back from the ghetto, they make the change for each other sometimes.
But the last dead which I see it, was a name, Yuri Solles.
I don't know the guy.
I know he had a couple children.
And this guy, which is over here, he was going every morning.
You know, in the village, he went.
He killed him right before going into the ghetto.
I go to work, we see the guy's laying dead.
And he killed him.
Shot him.
Yeah.
Shot-- shot is just like a fly, for them to shot a Jew.
What kind of apartment I had.
I had my house.
I had a room like this.
Was me and my mother and my brother.
And the other woman living there, my mother's sister, with the sister-- the sister, and the sister's husband, and their child, in the other room.
That's all it is.
But--
Yeah, she was-- no, before she was not my wife before.
Later on-- later on.
Before I was hided in the hole.
I married, it was probably-- she knows.
Maybe she's going to say the doctor-- what's his name?
Volcan.
The last day which I see her she left the city.
She was-- that time I was married.
Probably was by September-- don't remember exactly.
October or something, October.
But she is going to-- but you're going to write it.
She probably is living.
She's going to tell you exactly.
This, I tell you, about September, October.
I think October.
It was 1941-- '41. '41. 1941, by the Russians. 1941.
About October '41.
Before the ghetto by the Nazis-- by the Russians.
No, no.
Later on, later on.
When the Germans come, I was liberated.
I have nothing to do with the Russians when the Germans come.
Didn't I tell you?
No.
Let me tell you about my wife.
Saturday, was two days, three days before September-- August 26, 27.
We went to work, to lunch, all the 12 people.
We had to have lunch.
We wanted to get out from the ghetto.
Then the police-- the ghetto was already that police.
No more out.
Oh-- no more out.
So we have to finish the leather.
And the [NON-ENGLISH], Gretzner, he comes-- what's this [NON-ENGLISH] over here?
He says, what's-- you know what [NON-ENGLISH].
What's is doing over here?
He says, listen, Herr Gretzner, we have to finish.
We are finishing the work.
We started the work.
Tomorrow, I go later, and the leather will get spoiled.
He says, listen to me.
Why don't you let all this be?
What's the matter with you?
He says, you're the boss.
Then he goes with me, out the ghetto, and then from the ghetto out is a water, [NON-ENGLISH].
He comes to me.
He says, you hide yourself because it's terrible for you.
Tomorrow is going to be not for me.
Right now you do it yourself.
The opposite.
Use your sense.
I had with me a pair of soles.
Had a tie on.
He said give him a package, took away the package.
And you know the ghetto is closed, and they're bringing-- and we see-- another 20 people, another maybe from the forest, they're bringing in the ghetto.
And you know the ghetto, Saturday is closed.
We went up Saturday.
We start 5 o'clock.
Five people-- the other seven people remain inside.
And the children, our son, is with us.
I know something could happen.
They are in different places.
At night, their son was with us, and the grandfather and the father went away-- stealed away in the village we was born at, because we know later on.
We didn't know all the time.
And the son was with us.
[INAUDIBLE] 5 o'clock, we have to go home.
And I says, let me hide yourself.
We're going up in the chimney.
We had a big chimney which has got the brick.
And the chimney didn't work.
We made a space to live there.
We put on [INAUDIBLE] everything else.
Could be 10, 15 people.
It's a very big chimney.
But we figured in case they find out we're there, they make fire, and we're finished.
Then we run away from the chimney.
Other places we hide in wood.
And one guy says, with their son, says I don't believe they're going to kill us-- impossible.
How could you kill us?
The leather-- so much leathers for this.
He says, give me your name.
I gave him my name.
I give my this.
I give him other things with the son.
And then will the other guys, we're planning to go back to the forest.
We didn't go back to the forest, because it was very late.
We did hide ourselves.
In the factory, there are big woods-- like the woods is like over here, so much wood we have.
We put into the woods and made a hole, and put in water, put in bread, put pickles.
We had a lot of pickles because the garden-- I made pickles for myself.
We had tomatoes.
We says, we could stay a week, even a month.
And suddenly, he had the police-- 12 o'clock, they come to the guy.
He says, where are the other people?
He says, they went into the forest.
But I don't know.
We went in the roof.
We went to this.
He's showing the places.
He doesn't know where he is.
He's right near the woods.
He shoot them.
He shoots his guy right near the woods.
We see that.
He shoots them there in the woods, too.
Then the other guy says to me, don't shoot in the wood, because this is because you could make a fire.
But he says this-- then feel better.
This way we had over here, they're not pain.
You feel that you're drinking some bitter stone-- bitter, over here, yet so dark, so bitter in the eyes.
I look in the other eyes.
He couldn't see.
The eyes jumping terrible.
And they're going away.
Sunday come.
Other people up in the [INAUDIBLE], this is the place with the father and the son.
At night, we start to run away from there, which is danger.
We start to run away, start a new life.
We go to a village, a little village.
We start two guys, two brothers, Ukrainian, brothers.
But--
That was September 1 or 29 of August, because it was two days before Rosh Hashanah.
This is 1942.
Yeah, yeah.
Yeah.
She went out from the ghetto.
A lady over here, they're planning together.
I prepared her a place in the ghetto.
Also, in the ghetto we lived, the factory at work.
Prepared her a place with the baby, 6 months old.
Yes.
Nechama.
And prepared her a place.
[INAUDIBLE] And she could stay there, and I'm going to be-- and I'm going to be there.
And she has to go with that [NON-ENGLISH], with that lady which is in New York, right here.
She planned it to go with her.
And she has a girl for 8, 9, years.
That was 11 o'clock at night, still early, and the police didn't sleep.
They got on police to board it-- up.
And she said, the baby's sleeping, the-- I'm going to go and come back again.
She went home, and she went over the ghetto, and she paid off the police.
She went over the ghetto-- this [NON-ENGLISH].
She went over the ghetto, and she went back home to give her a drink and to sleeping.
And that's what she told me over here after the war.
I went in Germany.
She says to me, what's happened?
You have been planning with your wife.
I've been planning to hide myself.
And she says she has a place, by [NON-ENGLISH] the Ukrainish.
[NON-ENGLISH] We was hiding in the hole which was in the wood.
And this guy bring the police, and he took the leather.
He was the shower.
If not he, those two people wouldn't get killed.
It's the guy that killed them.
That brings out the point.
Then in the wartime, the money doesn't mean a thing.
If you give somebody-- a goy you give money, then you right away buy a death.
This lesson I had from a guy, which he was very smart.
He was Elze Gellar from [NON-ENGLISH].
This guy was very smart.
He was a Judenrat man.
And he says, you give a guy money, or leather, or anything else you buy, death.
He's going to kill you, whether he's going to report to the police, and that's what happened.
Other things I learned from Elze Gellar, which he says how to run away from the bullets, in case they shoot me in the back.
I did.
It's happened to me.
And a few things, whatever I learned from him.
[BACKROUND NOISE] He was in [NON-ENGLISH] progrom.
They kill your parents in Russia in 1917.
He tell me that story.
That means you have to learn what should happen.
Then later on, you're going to show it.
I learned a lot from him.
And how to save-- when they give you death penalty, about the leather spoiling, I didn't tell you yet.
I didn't tell you yet.
That was in 1942.
And I got the death penalty for leather, not-- because the people was hiding in the leather.
We had not give them the right material to get skin off.
Then I got from the [NON-ENGLISH] city Horochow.
I got death penalty.
And nobody could do anything.
They have to kill me.
Then they kept two, three-- two and a half weeks.
Then they kill me.
Then the [NON-ENGLISH]--
In Lokacze, 1942.
It was in-- it was in June 1942.
And he says there, he has the death penalty, and the death penalty is on.
But they didn't do it.
They says, I need a new man.
A man to me-- can't get a man like this.
How to get it?
Then the [NON-ENGLISH] said, try to get a man, because I want to kill him.
But the death penalty is laying in the table that I get.
Anyway, then they called 150 gypsies, 150-- forest.
They brought 150 gypsies in the back of the tannery, they had a big garden, as bigger as this house.
They bring-- in the garden I had pickle.
I had everything there-- tomatoes, and I had trees with cherries.
And they bring in 150 gypsies.
They are death penalty.
It was 2 o'clock.
Come to me the [NON-ENGLISH] person, who says, this is death penalty.
We have to go down together.
Shows the order.
You have to go with these.
I went with the gypsies together.
It was in the corner from that wall, which we have-- in the corner wall, we have a bell-- a-- a thing, hole, which you adding some leather, and we planning out 2 o'clock till 5 o'clock.
I have time-- four hours to finish the letter.
I didn't finish it.
You know the way it is.
Then they was watching me.
And we're planning.
That-- I'm going to be in that corner, and this corner we make all 2 o'clock at night, and we're going to get out.
But right now you have to go to the gypsies.
I went down there, and I was standing in the corner wall. 5 o'clock, 6 o'clock in the evening, comes out that guy over here, takes X-ray.
That was the law.
Take X-ray from the people before they got killed, and later X-ray when they got killed.
Is the picture in Germany someplace.
I was standing with a [NON-ENGLISH] with a white shirt and a [NON-ENGLISH] like this, and I was in the corner wall, which we're planning to make that all should get out. 12 o'clock--
How feel?
Over here I feel-- not the pain.
I told you about.
You feel [NON-ENGLISH].
You know what [NON-ENGLISH]?
Bitter-- terrible.
And the eyes is blanking.
I feel the sun is light and beautiful.
You feel just blank.
And actually, we understood each other with a wire.
I got on this corner.
They make the X-ray-- one X-ray, second X-ray.
And 12 o'clock at night comes the [NON-ENGLISH] Gretzner.
He comes to change the Ukrainish-- five, six people each, he comes to change.
[SPEAKING NON-ENGLISH] He says, in Danish, could I have a drink?
I was very thirsty.
So yeah, listen.
[SPEAKING NON-ENGLISH] Hide yourself.
[SPEAKING NON-ENGLISH] He yells to me, [SPEAKING NON-ENGLISH].
You know what this is?
Dirty Jew.
And I'm listening to that.
[SPEAKING NON-ENGLISH] And I go up and hide myself.
He takes away the six guys that were there, and the other one they didn't know about it.
Anyway, whatever it is, he was the boss.
And I had the keys.
On the place where they hide it, I know they are on the top. 12:00, 12:30, I went to them.
I had the keys.
I went up them.
We were laying and watching.
In the front was-- the roof, a little two windows, we see the gypsies.
But the reason we hide it because we are afraid they're going to steal us.
You know that.
And then comes the death penalty for me.
There was the second part. 2 o'clock was the death penalty. 5 o'clock I have to go to them.
Everything-- I went to them.
I went with them till 12 o'clock at night, and I was hiding.
And I was looking.
They took the pictures from them.
And so they shooed all of them out in the back.
We was laying in the room.
Because we're not allowed to be out, 9 o'clock at night.
We're laying in the room.
We're laying in the room.
[NON-ENGLISH] That's all-- five people. 5 o'clock comes from the other people, and they're buried under-- not far from there, they're buried under-- all the 50 people.
And the state-- then comes two of them.
After the death, after they go, comes two of them.
This one guy goes-- wants to shoot them.
[NON-ENGLISH] says no.
That's finished, [NON-ENGLISH], Ukrainish swine.
[NON-ENGLISH] --because you have to make an X-ray list, and those two has the picture.
Put all of this-- [NON-ENGLISH].
Let him go.
They went away.
They went away, and that's it.
But later on, when Bandera came out with the thing to make independent national Ukraine against the Russians, against this, and the Jewish, no question about it.
[INAUDIBLE] Then this guy, one from the gypsies, I meet him there to work, because I was the manager from the party, from the-- they called it-- was the boss from the tannery, and I need workers.
I took this guy sometimes to work, to cut wood, something like this.
I spoke to him.
How did you manage it?
The other one is dead, and I'm alive.
That's it.
Another story.
It's a big story.
We'll stop here.
